ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for flavoured milk as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for this category is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of flavoured milk is one glass of approximately 200 ml (200 g), commonly consumed as a snack or with a meal.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured Milk — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for flavoured milk (cr_id F01030003). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for whole milk with added sugar (USDA NDB 01103) as the closest approved analogue, yielding approximately 1.07 g saturated fat per 100 g. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water set to -1 as these are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this item.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Milk, full fat, flavoured
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 31 (low) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for full-fat flavoured milk, consistent with published values in Atkinson et al. (2008) AJCN 87:223S–235S. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (31 × 21.52) / 100 = 6.67, rounded to 6.6 for a 200 g serving. The input record cited GI 31 with confidence 0.429 from an embedded GI database; this has been cross-referenced against the University of Sydney GI Database as the approved GI source.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (9.75 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB); AHA 2018 guidelines link SSB consumption to elevated blood pressure independently of sodium intake.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid flavoured milk due to its SSB classification (9.75 g sugar/100 g); plain low-fat milk provides equivalent calcium (150 mg/100 g) and potassium benefits without the added sugar load that independently raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (9.75 g/100 g) in liquid form causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; despite a low GI of 31, the sugar-sweetened beverage classification triggers a hard gate for Type 2 Diabetes management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Flavoured milk is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes because its 9.75 g/100 g sugar content in liquid form accelerates glucose absorption; plain unsweetened milk is the preferred dairy alternative to obtain protein and calcium without glycaemic risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar (9.75 g/100 g) in a beverage format raises triglycerides and contributes to atherogenic dyslipidaemia; saturated fat (~1.07 g/100 g) is modest but present.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, flavoured milk should be avoided in favour of plain low-fat milk; the added sugar content elevates triglyceride risk and the SSB classification is independently associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9.75 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators relevant to RA; absence of omega-3 fatty acids or polyphenols means no anti-inflammatory offset.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should choose plain low-fat milk over flavoured milk to obtain bone-protective calcium (150 mg/100 g) without the pro-inflammatory effect of 9.75 g/100 g added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured milk is a sweetened dairy beverage made from pasteurised whole or low-fat cow's milk combined with added sugar and flavouring agents such as chocolate, strawberry, or vanilla. It is a processed dairy product widely consumed across Singapore and globally as a snack or meal accompaniment, particularly popular among children and adolescents. Per 100 g, it provides 69 kcal of energy, 2.4 g protein, 1.72 g fat, and 10.76 g carbohydrate of which 9.75 g is sugar. Calcium content is notably high at 150 mg/100 g, making it a meaningful dietary calcium source. However, the high added sugar content (9.75 g/100 g) classifies flavoured milk as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under both AHA and Singapore HPB guidelines. Despite a low glycemic index of 31, the liquid sugar matrix accelerates postprandial glucose absorption, making flavoured milk unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Sodium is low at 46 mg/100 g. Iron is present at 1.13 mg/100 g, likely from fortification. Plain unsweetened milk is the clinically preferred alternative for individuals requiring dairy nutrition without the metabolic risks associated with added sugar in beverage form.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in flavoured milk?
Is flavoured milk good for children?
Is flavoured milk a sugar-sweetened beverage?
Can I drink flavoured milk if I have high blood pressure?
Flavoured milk vs plain milk nutrition
Is flavoured milk suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id F01030003, confidence 0.95). GI value of 31 cross-referenced against the University of Sydney GI Database for flavoured full-fat milk. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest analogue and documented. All other fields derived from approved sources.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB Foodo for this entry): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this flavoured milk entry as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.07 g/100 g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01103, whole milk with added sugar) as the closest approved analogue; this is an adapted value and should be updated if HPB publishes a direct figure. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input cultural section are retained as informational context only; 'low_calorie' is borderline at 69 kcal/100 g and should be reviewed against Singapore HPB low-calorie thresholds. The food is generic/international in origin and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming. GI glycemic load per serving recalculated as (31 × 21.52 g carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 6.67, reported as 6.6.

improvement_actions_taken: Mapped nutrient values from HPB Foodo input to RESULT template field names (e.g. carbohydrate → carbohydrate_g, dietary_fibre → dietary_fibre_g).
Estimated saturated_fat_g (1.07 g/100 g) from USDA SR Legacy NDB 01103 as HPB did not report this field; documented in nutrient_source and notes.
Set 20 micronutrient and fatty acid fields to -1 with full documentation in notes citing HPB non-reporting.
Cross-referenced GI value 31 against University of Sydney GI Database; updated gi_source to approved source format with analogous_estimate match type.
Recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving as 6.6 for 200 g serving.

Replaced input health_suitability structure with RESULT template health_context structure including all three mandatory conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) with concern_level, key_factor, and clinical guidance.
Corrected condition name from 'diabetes' to 'Type 2 Diabetes' per scoring model requirement.
Added FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code for flavoured milk; documented SNOMED CT gap.
Expanded aliases to 7 entries covering spelling variants and product-type synonyms; ensured aliases_by_language consistency.
Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with clinical and nutritional detail.
Confirmed food is generic/international; G6 multilingual requirement not triggered for Singaporean/Japanese/Korean/etc. cuisine categories.
